output.answer:
SOP-FIN-002 **partially meets** SR 11-7’s governance and model inventory expectations, but it needs stronger, explicit **model inventory content requirements** and cleanup of some **validation/independence ambiguities**.

## Assessment against SR 11-7

| SR 11-7 expectation | SOP-FIN-002 evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk-based model risk management appropriate to model use and business risk.** SR 11-7 states that organizations are responsible for adopting model risk management practices appropriate to the specific risks they face, and that the guidance is especially relevant to models supporting significant business lines, operations, services, and functions. *(SR 11-7, Sections I–II, Introduction / Purpose and Scope)* | The SOP applies to a fraud detection system supporting approximately **$4.2B annually** and includes ML fraud scoring, rules, alerting, retraining, monitoring, escalation, and governance. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§1.1–1.2, 5.2, 7)* | **Mostly meets.** The system is material enough to warrant SR 11-7-style governance, and the SOP recognizes the model as operationally significant. |
| **Governance supported by clear policies and effective controls.** SR 11-7 says all model lifecycle components benefit from governance supported by clear policies and controls; policies should define risk management expectations and a framework for assessing model risk and applying controls commensurate with risk. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Governance and Controls)* | The SOP has a model governance policy requiring initial and annual model risk assessments, validation reports, Model Governance Committee review, change approval, performance monitoring, retraining, decommissioning, controls, exceptions, and reporting. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§4.2, 5.2.2–5.2.3, 6, 7, 8)* | **Mostly meets, with a gap.** The SOP says annual model risk assessments occur, but it does not define the model risk rating methodology, materiality tiers, or how governance rigor is calibrated to model risk. It references SOP-AI-001, but SR 11-7 expectations should be explicitly reflected or cross-referenced with enough detail. |
| **Clear roles, responsibilities, and accountability across the model lifecycle.** SR 11-7 expects clear roles and responsibilities, well-defined accountability, and delineation of individuals responsible for key lifecycle activities from development through validation and monitoring. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Roles and Responsibilities)* | The SOP includes a RACI matrix, role descriptions for Fraud Operations, Data Science, Platform Engineering, Compliance, VP Financial Services, and the Model Governance Committee. It assigns responsibilities for model retraining, performance monitoring, deployment approval, decommissioning, validation, and reporting. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§3.1–3.2, 5.2, 7.3)* | **Mostly meets.** Accountability is generally clear. |
| **Independence and conflict-of-interest controls for validation / effective challenge.** SR 11-7 states sound model risk management involves “effective challenge” by objective experts with appropriate expertise, sufficient independence, and organizational standing. It also notes governance should address conflicts of interest between model development and validation groups. *(SR 11-7, Section III / Effective Challenge; Section VI, Roles and Responsibilities)* | The SOP states validation findings are documented and presented to the Model Governance Committee; validation may not be performed by individuals directly involved in model development in the current cycle; COI disclosures are required; and segregation of duties prohibits Fraud Data Science/model developers from model validation. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§4.2, 6.2 FDS-AC-004, 6.3)* | **Partial.** The independence principle is present, but the SOP is internally inconsistent: the Fraud Data Science role description says the team “executes model validation activities,” while §6.3 prohibits Fraud Data Science from model validation. Also, §3.2 references validation “as defined in Section 5.4,” but §5.4 is false-positive management, not validation. This weakens the governance design. |
| **Internal audit should evaluate model risk management effectiveness rather than duplicate development or validation.** SR 11-7 says internal audit, where part of model risk management, generally evaluates whether model risk management practices are rigorous/effective and implemented, rather than duplicating development or validation. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Roles and Responsibilities)* | The SOP requires an annual FDS control effectiveness assessment by Internal Audit or a qualified independent assessor. *(SOP-FIN-002 §6.2 FDS-AC-003)* | **Meets.** The SOP positions audit as an assessor of control effectiveness. |
| **Oversight of external resources / third parties used in model risk management.** SR 11-7 states that when external resources help manage model risk, sound practice includes proper oversight and integration into broader model risk management activities, with clearly defined roles and responsibilities. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Roles and Responsibilities; Section VII, Vendor and Other Third-Party Products)* | The SOP covers third-party model validation consultants, AWS, external fraud databases, annual due diligence, independence from development teams, and COI review before engagement. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§3.1, 6.4)* | **Mostly meets.** Oversight exists, though the SOP could better require integration of third-party validation findings into the model inventory and remediation tracking. |
| **Comprehensive model inventory with enough information to understand model risks at individual and aggregate levels.** SR 11-7 describes it as common industry practice to maintain a comprehensive set of information for models under development or in use, sufficient to understand model risks and support risk management at individual and aggregate levels. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Model Inventory)* | The SOP says the Model Governance Committee maintains Meridian’s enterprise model inventory and an inventory of all active fraud detection models. It also maintains a detailed **rule** inventory, but not a detailed **model** inventory. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§3.2, 4.2, 5.1.2)* | **Gap / partial.** The rule inventory is well specified, but the ML model inventory is not. The SOP does not require inventory fields such as model owner, developer, validator, intended use, business line, model version, status, implementation date, risk/materiality rating, limitations, assumptions, data sources, dependencies, validation date/status, monitoring results, open issues, exceptions, remediation items, or retirement status. Without those fields, the inventory may not be sufficient to understand model risk at individual and aggregate levels as expected by SR 11-7. |
| **Documentation supports continuity, tracking of recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation.** SR 11-7 says adequate documentation supports continuity of operations and tracking of recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation efforts. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Documentation)* | The SOP requires model validation reports, model comparison reports, exception forms, exception retrospectives, rule change documentation, dashboards, monthly/quarterly reports, and archived exception records. *(SOP-FIN-002 §§4.2, 5.1.1, 5.2.2, 7.3, 8.2)* | **Mostly meets.** Documentation is strong operationally, but remediation tracking for model validation findings is not explicitly tied to the model inventory or an issue-management process. |

## Key gaps to remediate

1. **Define a formal model inventory schema.**  
   Add a “Model Inventory and Documentation” subsection for ML models, not just rules. At minimum, require fields for:
   - Unique model ID and name/version;
   - Model owner, developer, user/business owner, validator;
   - Business purpose and approved use;
   - Model type/methodology;
   - In-scope systems and dependencies;
   - Data sources and critical inputs;
   - Model risk/materiality rating;
   - Key assumptions and limitations;
   - Validation status, validation date, validator, and next validation due date;
   - Performance monitoring metrics and thresholds;
   - Open findings, remediation plans, exceptions, and issue owners;
   - Deployment status, approval date, production date, and retirement/decommissioning date.

   This is needed because SR 11-7 expects an inventory containing sufficient information to understand model risks at individual and aggregate levels. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Model Inventory)*

2. **Clarify validation independence.**  
   Revise the RACI and role descriptions so Fraud Data Science develops, monitors, and supports validation, but does **not** independently validate models it developed. The current text conflicts: §3.2 says Fraud Data Science executes validation, while §6.3 prohibits Fraud Data Science/model developers from validation. SR 11-7 expects effective challenge by objective experts with sufficient independence. *(SR 11-7, Section III / Effective Challenge; Section VI, Roles and Responsibilities)*

3. **Add explicit model risk tiering/materiality criteria.**  
   The SOP says model risk assessments occur at deployment and annually, but it should define how HML-FM is risk-rated and how validation depth, monitoring frequency, approval level, and reporting cadence scale with risk. SR 11-7 expects policies to establish a framework for assessing model risk and applying model risk management practices commensurate with that risk. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Governance and Controls)*

4. **Tie validation findings to remediation and inventory tracking.**  
   The SOP requires validation reports, but it should require documented management responses, issue owners, due dates, severity ratings, exception approvals, and closure evidence. SR 11-7 notes documentation should support tracking of recommendations, responses, exceptions, and remediation. *(SR 11-7, Section VI, Documentation)*

## Bottom line

SOP-FIN-002 has a solid governance foundation: defined roles, committee oversight, monitoring, retraining, controls, reporting, COI controls, and third-party oversight. However, it does **not fully meet SR 11-7 inventory expectations** because the model inventory requirements are only stated at a high level and lack the content needed to understand and manage model risk. It also needs cleanup around validation independence.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-AIML-006 (Model Performance Monitoring) compliant with SR 11-7's three foundational elements?
question.expected_answer: Gap. Despite addressing monitoring at the technical level, the SOP fails on the governance dimension: no escalation path for model-risk findings to the board/senior management (SR 11-7 Section V), no challenger-model expectation for high-impact decisions (Section III — effective challenge), and no overarching model risk management framework or model inventory tying monitoring back to the bank's model population. These are core SR 11-7 structures and their absence is non-compliance, not partial.

output.answer:
No. **SOP-AIML-006 is partially compliant** with SR 11-7’s three foundational elements. It is strong for **ongoing monitoring and operational controls**, but it does not, by itself, fully satisfy SR 11-7’s broader expectations for **model development/implementation/use**, **independent validation/effective challenge**, and **enterprise model governance**.

| SR 11-7 foundational element | SR 11-7 expectation | SOP-AIML-006 assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Model development, implementation, and use** | SR 11-7 expects model development and use to be aligned with model purpose, business use, and policy; development should include a clear purpose, appropriate data/methodology/testing, user input, and controls over use beyond intended purpose. See **SR 11-7 §IV, Model Development and Model Use**. | **Partial.** SOP-AIML-006 requires model registry metadata, business purpose, owner, training dataset reference, baseline metrics, data distribution, SLAs, and dashboards before production/shadow deployment (**SOP §§5.1–5.2**). It also monitors use conditions through drift, latency, traffic, and performance alerts (**SOP §§5.2–5.4**). However, it expressly excludes pre-deployment validation and acceptance testing (**SOP §1.3**) and does not define controls for validating intended use, assumptions, model limitations, implementation verification, or use outside original purpose, which SR 11-7 treats as part of sound model use and development controls (**SR 11-7 §IV**). |
| **2. Model validation** | SR 11-7 states validation should assess whether models perform as expected, identify limitations and errors, clarify appropriate use, and determine whether corrective action is warranted; outcomes analysis compares model outputs to real-world outcomes, and ongoing monitoring evaluates performance under changing products, exposures, activities, clients, data relevance, or market conditions. See **SR 11-7 §V, Model Validation and Monitoring**. | **Partial to strong for monitoring, but incomplete for validation.** SOP-AIML-006 has detailed outcomes/performance monitoring: AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, F1, RMSE, ROUGE/BLEU, prediction drift, data drift, delayed-label pipelines, ground-truth validation, alert thresholds, RCA, rollback, fallback, and sunsetting escalation (**SOP §§5.2–5.5, 6.1–6.2, 8.2**). These align well with SR 11-7’s ongoing monitoring and outcomes-analysis expectations (**SR 11-7 §V**). The gap is that SR 11-7 validation also expects independent, objective challenge of model reliability, limitations, assumptions, and conceptual soundness; SOP-AIML-006 assigns monitoring largely to Model Owners, MLOps, Engineering, and product stakeholders, but does not require independent validation review or independent approval of monitoring thresholds, incidents, recalibration, or continued model use (**SR 11-7 §§III, V**; **SOP §§5.4–5.5, 6.1, 7.2**). |
| **3. Governance, policies, and controls** | SR 11-7 expects model governance supported by clear policies and effective controls, risk management expectations, roles and responsibilities, accountability, monitoring/control processes, resource allocation, documentation, model inventory, oversight of third-party models, and internal audit or similar review without duplicating model development/validation. See **SR 11-7 §VI, Governance and Controls** and **§VII, Vendor and Other Third-Party Products**. | **Partial.** SOP-AIML-006 includes useful governance controls: named Model Owner, MLOps responsibilities, dashboards, access control, alert thresholds as code, automated rollback gates, incident logging, post-mortems, exception handling, escalation, KPIs, reporting cadence, and training (**SOP §§5.1, 5.4–5.5, 6.1–6.4, 7.1–7.2, 8.1–8.2, 9**). It also covers third-party/procured models in scope (**SOP §1.2**). But it does not establish a full SR 11-7 governance framework: there is no explicit enterprise model risk tiering/materiality framework, no independent model risk management function, no internal audit review role, no formal conflict-of-interest controls between development, monitoring, and validation, and no detailed third-party/vendor validation oversight process for proprietary vendor models (**SR 11-7 §§VI–VII**). |

## Key compliance strengths

- **Ongoing monitoring is well developed.** SOP-AIML-006 continuously monitors performance, data drift, prediction drift, latency, traffic, and missing features, which supports SR 11-7’s expectation that ongoing monitoring evaluate whether a model continues to perform as expected as conditions change (**SR 11-7 §V; SOP §§5.2–5.4**).
- **Outcomes analysis is addressed.** The SOP links predictions to ground truth, includes delayed-label handling, and defines model-type-specific performance metrics, which aligns with SR 11-7’s outcomes-analysis concept of comparing model outputs to real-world outcomes (**SR 11-7 §V; SOP §§5.2.1–5.2.2**).
- **Corrective action mechanisms exist.** Rollback, traffic shunting, feature-off controls, RCA, post-mortems, and model sunset escalation align with SR 11-7’s expectation that performance deterioration may require overlays, adjustment, redevelopment, or other corrective action (**SR 11-7 §V; SOP §§5.5, 6.1, 8.2**).
- **Operational governance is mature.** The SOP defines alerting, reporting, access control, exception handling, escalation, and training, which supports SR 11-7’s governance/control expectations for monitoring and control processes (**SR 11-7 §VI; SOP §§6–9**).

## Material gaps to remediate

1. **Add independent validation / effective challenge.**  
   SR 11-7 expects effective challenge by objective experts with sufficient independence, expertise, and authority across the lifecycle, including ongoing monitoring (**SR 11-7 §III**). SOP-AIML-006 should require independent Model Risk Management or Validation review of monitoring design, thresholds, material incidents, recalibration decisions, and continued-use decisions.

2. **Add explicit model limitations monitoring.**  
   SR 11-7 states that ongoing monitoring should regularly assess model limitations over time and include procedures for responding to issues before and after approval for use (**SR 11-7 §V**). SOP-AIML-006 monitors performance and drift but does not require periodic reassessment of documented limitations, assumptions, intended-use boundaries, or prohibited-use conditions.

3. **Tie monitoring rigor to model materiality/risk tier.**  
   SR 11-7 expects practices to be commensurate with model risk, complexity, materiality, and business use (**SR 11-7 §§III, V–VI**). SOP-AIML-006 distinguishes some model categories and high-risk use cases, but it does not define risk-tiered monitoring frequency, approval authority, escalation thresholds, or independent review requirements.

4. **Strengthen governance/accountability and conflict controls.**  
   SR 11-7 emphasizes clear roles, responsibilities, accountability, and controls over conflicts of interest between development and validation functions (**SR 11-7 §VI**). SOP-AIML-006 identifies Model Owner, MLOps, Engineering, and executives, but does not define a RACI including Model Risk Management, Compliance, Internal Audit, or independent validation.

5. **Expand third-party model oversight.**  
   SR 11-7 recognizes vendor and third-party models as presenting unique validation and risk-management challenges (**SR 11-7 §VII**). SOP-AIML-006 includes third-party/procured models in scope, but lacks procedures for vendor performance evidence, contractual access to monitoring data, independent vendor-model validation, issue escalation to vendors, and proprietary-model limitation documentation.

## Bottom line

SOP-AIML-006 is a solid **model performance monitoring SOP**, and it substantially supports the **monitoring** portion of SR 11-7. However, SR 11-7’s three foundational elements are broader than operational monitoring. Because the SOP excludes pre-deployment validation, lacks independent validation/effective challenge, and does not fully define enterprise model governance controls, it is **not fully compliant on a standalone basis**.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-CLIN-005 (Diagnostic AI Accuracy and Calibration) satisfy NIST AI RMF's foundational expectations?
question.expected_answer: Gap. The SOP addresses accuracy and calibration metrics in detail but is silent on the surrounding framework: (1) no AI risk management framework adopted or referenced (GOVERN absent); (2) no AI system inventory mapping the diagnostic AI to organisational risk (MAP absent); (3) no bias/fairness/equity testing methodology beyond accuracy — a critical gap for diagnostic AI given demographic disparities. Accuracy alone is one MEASURE characteristic among seven; missing the framework around it is a material gap.

output.answer:
SOP-CLIN-005 **partially satisfies** NIST AI RMF’s foundational expectations. It is strong on **validity, reliability, calibration, clinical safety, human oversight, independent validation, monitoring, and subgroup performance**, but it does not fully cover the NIST AI RMF’s broader foundational expectations for **enterprise governance, privacy-enhanced AI, full socio-technical impact mapping, residual-risk management, secure/resilient operations, and stakeholder/community feedback loops**.

## Basis: NIST AI RMF expectations

NIST AI RMF 1.0 frames trustworthy AI around characteristics including **valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair with harmful bias managed**. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Part 1, Section 3, “AI Risks and Trustworthiness,” and Executive Summary/Page 2.

NIST AI RMF’s Core functions are **GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, and MANAGE**; GOVERN applies across organizational AI risk management processes, while MAP, MEASURE, and MANAGE apply in AI-system-specific contexts and lifecycle stages. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Part 2, Section 5, “AI RMF Core,” and Executive Summary/Page 2.

NIST also emphasizes that AI systems should be tested before deployment and regularly while in operation, with test/evaluation/verification/validation processes, uncertainty measures, benchmarks, documentation, and independent review. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.3, “MEASURE,” Table 3.

## Assessment against foundational expectations

| NIST AI RMF expectation | SOP-CLIN-005 assessment |
|---|---|
| **Valid and reliable** | **Strongly addressed.** The SOP requires pre-specified clinical performance thresholds, independent validation, held-out testing, external validation datasets, confidence intervals, calibration curves, ECE gates, CITL, calibration slope, and degradation testing. This aligns well with NIST’s expectation that AI risks and trustworthy characteristics be measured through documented TEVV methods, metrics, test sets, and tools. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.1 “Valid and Reliable”; Section 5.3 “MEASURE”; Table 3, MEASURE 1.1, 2.1, 2.3. |
| **Safe** | **Mostly addressed.** The SOP focuses on clinical safety through release gates, CMO approval, human oversight, silent mode, adverse-event summaries in QBRs, low-confidence suppression, and input validation. This aligns with NIST’s safe-AI trustworthiness characteristic and NIST’s expectation that organizations make go/no-go deployment decisions and manage negative impacts. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.2 “Safe”; Section 4 “Effectiveness of the AI RMF”; Section 5.4 “MANAGE,” Table 4, MANAGE 1.1. |
| **Fair with harmful bias managed** | **Substantially addressed, but not complete.** The SOP requires subgroup definitions, subgroup performance metrics, statistical and clinical significance thresholds, root-cause analysis, and mitigation plans. This supports NIST’s fair/bias-managed characteristic. However, it is primarily performance-parity focused and does not require broader bias analysis of data sourcing, labeling processes, access disparities, deployment context, or affected-community engagement. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.7 “Fair – with Harmful Bias Managed”; Section 5.2 “MAP,” Table 2, MAP 5.1–5.2; Section 5.3, Table 3, MEASURE 1.2–1.3. |
| **Explainable and interpretable** | **Partially to mostly addressed.** The SOP requires clinician-facing confidence scores in natural language or intuitive visual formats and a human-factors workflow evaluation. This supports interpretability for clinical users. However, it does not require documentation of explanation methods, explanation limitations, model behavior summaries, or validation that explanations are faithful to the model. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.5 “Explainable and Interpretable”; Section 5.3, Table 3, MEASURE 2.1–2.3. |
| **Accountable and transparent** | **Partially addressed.** The SOP has a RACI matrix, separation between developers and validators, QA review, release authorization, immutable audit logs, training records, exception handling, and QMS documentation. These support accountability and transparency. Gaps remain because the SOP does not define external transparency artifacts, stakeholder disclosures, model cards/system cards, public-facing limitations, or mechanisms for downstream acquirers and users to understand residual risks. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.4 “Accountable and Transparent”; Section 4 “Effectiveness of the AI RMF”; Section 5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.4. |
| **Secure and resilient** | **Partially addressed.** The SOP includes an adversarial input filter, input integrity guard, production artifact access control, audit logging, latency/error-rate monitoring, and incident triggers. However, it does not define a full AI security program: threat modeling, red-teaming, vulnerability management, model supply-chain controls, secure SDLC integration, recovery/rollback criteria, or resilience testing. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.3 “Secure and Resilient”; Section 5.2, Table 2, MAP 4.1–4.2; Section 5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 2.3. |
| **Privacy-enhanced** | **Gap.** The SOP mentions hashed patient IDs and HIPAA-compliant logs, but it does not establish privacy-enhancing controls for training, validation, monitoring, data minimization, consent/authorization, de-identification, re-identification risk, privacy impact assessments, retention, or secondary-use governance. NIST explicitly identifies “privacy-enhanced” as a trustworthy AI characteristic. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.6 “Privacy-Enhanced”; Executive Summary/Page 2. |
| **GOVERN function** | **Partial.** The SOP has role accountability, training, QA review, release authorization, and exception management. But NIST’s GOVERN function is broader and applies across all stages of organizational AI risk management; the SOP is a model-accuracy/calibration SOP and does not by itself define enterprise AI risk tolerance, portfolio-level governance, risk appetite, policy hierarchy, third-party governance, or enterprise integration. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.1 “GOVERN”; Executive Summary/Page 2. |
| **MAP function** | **Partial.** The SOP maps intended clinical use, patient population, ground truth, subgroups, deployment environments, and clinical workflow. But it does not comprehensively map impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society; does not require likelihood/magnitude scoring for impacts; and does not require regular engagement with external stakeholders or affected communities. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.2 “MAP,” Table 2, MAP 3.4–3.5, MAP 4.1–4.2, MAP 5.1–5.2. |
| **MEASURE function** | **Strong.** This is the SOP’s strongest area. It has documented metrics, test sets, independent validation, uncertainty estimates, benchmark thresholds, performance monitoring, calibration monitoring, subgroup analysis, and periodic reassessment. This aligns closely with NIST’s MEASURE function. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.3 “MEASURE,” Table 3, MEASURE 1.1–1.3 and MEASURE 2.1–2.3. |
| **MANAGE function** | **Partial.** The SOP includes deployment gates, failure reviews, recalibration, rollout pauses, exception approvals, and monitoring escalation. But it lacks a complete risk-treatment framework for all identified AI risks, residual-risk documentation to downstream users, non-AI alternative assessment, risk acceptance criteria, and formal prioritization based on impact/likelihood/resources. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.4 “MANAGE,” Table 4, MANAGE 1.1–1.4 and MANAGE 2.1–2.3. |

## Key strengths

1. **Robust clinical validation and calibration controls**  
   The SOP requires internal hold-out testing, independent validation, calibration metrics, confidence intervals, ECE gates, CITL, calibration slope, and paired degradation testing. This directly supports NIST’s expectation for documented, repeatable TEVV and performance measurement. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.3, Table 3, MEASURE 1.1, 2.1, 2.3.

2. **Clear go/no-go release controls**  
   The SOP prohibits clinical deployment without independent validation and formal release authorization. This supports NIST’s expectation for explicit commissioning/deployment decisions. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 4 “Effectiveness of the AI RMF”; Section 5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.1.

3. **Independent review and segregation of duties**  
   The Clinical Validation Lead is independent from the development team, and no single individual may hold contradictory roles. This aligns with NIST’s emphasis on independent assessors and internal experts not serving as front-line developers. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.3, Table 3, MEASURE 1.3.

4. **Lifecycle monitoring**  
   The SOP includes silent mode, continuous drift monitoring, monthly clinical monitoring, and quarterly quality review. NIST expects AI systems to be tested before deployment and regularly while in operation. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.3 “MEASURE.”

5. **Subgroup analysis and mitigation**  
   The SOP requires subgroup performance reporting, root-cause analysis, and mitigation plans. This supports NIST’s fairness and bias-management expectations. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.7; Section 5.2, Table 2, MAP 5.1–5.2.

## Material gaps to close

To better satisfy NIST AI RMF’s foundational expectations, SOP-CLIN-005 should add or cross-reference controls for:

1. **Privacy-enhanced AI**  
   Add requirements for privacy impact assessment, data minimization, de-identification standards, re-identification risk assessment, retention limits, patient-data governance, monitoring-data reuse rules, and privacy-preserving model development where appropriate. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.6 “Privacy-Enhanced.”

2. **Full MAP impact assessment**  
   Add a required AI Impact Assessment covering affected individuals, patient subgroups, clinicians, hospitals, downstream users, communities, foreseeable misuse, automation bias, access disparities, and benefit/harm likelihood and magnitude. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.2, Table 2, MAP 5.1.

3. **Stakeholder and affected-community feedback**  
   The SOP should require documented engagement with clinicians, patients or patient representatives, deployment-site operators, and other affected stakeholders, especially for high-risk clinical systems. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.2, Table 2, MAP 5.2; Section 5.3, Table 3, MEASURE 1.3.

4. **Residual-risk documentation and communication**  
   Add a requirement to document residual risks, accepted risks, limitations, contraindicated uses, subgroup caveats, and downstream-user disclosures before release. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.4.

5. **Risk prioritization and treatment methodology**  
   The SOP has failure triggers and exceptions, but it should define a formal risk scoring method using impact, likelihood, detectability, and available mitigations, with documented risk responses: mitigate, transfer, avoid, or accept. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.4, Table 4, MANAGE 1.2–1.3.

6. **Security and resilience program integration**  
   Add or cross-reference requirements for AI threat modeling, adversarial testing, model supply-chain controls, vulnerability management, red-team testing, rollback/recovery testing, and resilience validation. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 3.3 “Secure and Resilient”; Section 5.2, Table 2, MAP 4.1–4.2.

7. **Enterprise GOVERN alignment**  
   The SOP should explicitly link to enterprise AI risk appetite, governance committees, risk registers, third-party governance, regulatory obligations, and audit cadence. NIST’s GOVERN function applies across all stages of organizational AI risk management, not only model-level validation. NIST AI RMF 1.0, Section 5.1 “GOVERN”; Executive Summary/Page 2.

## Conclusion

SOP-CLIN-005 is a strong **model performance, calibration, clinical validation, and monitoring SOP**. It substantially supports NIST AI RMF’s **MEASURE** function and portions of **MANAGE**, and it addresses several trustworthy AI characteristics, especially **valid/reliable**, **safe**, and **fairness through subgroup performance**.

However, it does **not fully satisfy NIST AI RMF’s foundational expectations** because it leaves material gaps in **privacy-enhanced AI, enterprise governance, socio-technical impact mapping, residual-risk documentation, stakeholder engagement, and comprehensive security/resilience controls**.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Is SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) compliant with HIPAA's expectations for handling PHI referenced in regulatory filings?
question.expected_answer: Gap. SOP-LEGC-005 includes regulatory filings that may reference PHI but lacks foundational HIPAA controls: (1) no PHI de-identification methodology — neither Safe Harbor (§164.514(b)(2)) nor Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)) — for data appearing in filings; (2) breach-notification procedures are entirely missing — no reference to §164.400-414 timelines or required content; (3) no documented minimum-necessary policy (§164.502(b)) for the PHI that filings personnel may access. These are core HIPAA controls; their absence in a regulatory-filings SOP that touches PHI is a material gap.

output.answer:
No. **SOP-LEGC-005 is only partially compliant** with HIPAA expectations for PHI in regulatory filings.

### Key HIPAA expectations

HIPAA permits PHI disclosures only when permitted or required by the Privacy Rule. Covered entities may disclose PHI as permitted under **45 CFR § 164.502(a)(1)(vi)**, including disclosures under **45 CFR § 164.512**. HIPAA also permits disclosures to health oversight agencies for oversight activities authorized by law, including audits, investigations, inspections, licensure, disciplinary actions, and other oversight of the health care system or regulated entities where health information is necessary. **45 CFR § 164.512(d)(1)**.

HIPAA generally requires covered entities and business associates to make reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the **minimum necessary** for the purpose, except where the disclosure is “required by law” under **45 CFR § 164.512(a)** or required for HIPAA compliance. **45 CFR § 164.502(b)(1)-(2)**. For routine recurring disclosures, HIPAA requires policies or standard protocols limiting PHI to the amount reasonably necessary; for non-routine disclosures, it requires criteria and individual review. **45 CFR § 164.514(d)(3)**.

If Meridian acts as a **business associate**, it may use or disclose PHI only as permitted or required by its business associate agreement or as required by law, and not in a way that would violate HIPAA if done by the covered entity. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)(3)**.

For electronic PHI, HIPAA requires administrative and technical safeguards, including access control, audit controls, integrity protections, person/entity authentication, and transmission security. **45 CFR §§ 164.308(a)(1), 164.308(a)(4)-(6), 164.312(a)-(e)**. HIPAA also requires appropriate administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect PHI from intentional or unintentional impermissible use or disclosure. **45 CFR § 164.530(c)**.

### Where SOP-LEGC-005 aligns

SOP-LEGC-005 has some controls that support HIPAA-aligned handling of PHI in filings:

- It includes HIPAA within scope and defines PHI. SOP-LEGC-005 §§ 1.2, 2.
- It requires legal/compliance review for filings and final senior approval for material filings. SOP-LEGC-005 §§ 5.3, 4.4. This partially supports the need to ensure disclosures are legally permitted under HIPAA’s use/disclosure framework. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)**.
- It prohibits standard unencrypted email submissions unless approved by the CISO and GC, and requires use of designated regulatory portals. SOP-LEGC-005 § 5.4. This supports HIPAA transmission security expectations for ePHI. **45 CFR § 164.312(e)**.
- It maintains an official repository, audit trails, submission proof, metadata tagging, restricted edit access after submission, and access log review. SOP-LEGC-005 §§ 5.2, 5.4, 5.5, 6 C-03. These controls support audit and integrity expectations for ePHI systems. **45 CFR §§ 164.312(b), 164.312(c)**.
- It includes training requirements for personnel involved in filings. SOP-LEGC-005 § 9. HIPAA requires workforce training on relevant privacy policies and procedures. **45 CFR § 164.530(b)**.

### Material gaps

However, the SOP does **not** adequately address HIPAA-specific PHI handling in regulatory filings.

1. **No required HIPAA legal-basis determination before including PHI.**  
   The SOP does not require the RFO or Legal/Compliance reviewer to classify each PHI disclosure as required by law, permitted health oversight disclosure, disclosure to HHS/OCR, disclosure under authorization, de-identified disclosure, limited data set disclosure, or prohibited disclosure. HIPAA requires PHI uses/disclosures to fit within a permitted or required basis. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)**; health oversight disclosures are permitted only for oversight activities authorized by law. **45 CFR § 164.512(d)(1)**.

2. **No HIPAA minimum-necessary review.**  
   The SOP requires data integrity and accuracy review, but it does not require a documented minimum-necessary analysis before PHI is included in a filing. HIPAA requires reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary except for enumerated exceptions, including disclosures required by law. **45 CFR § 164.502(b)(1)-(2)**. For routine filings, the SOP should define standard protocols limiting PHI; for non-routine filings, it should require criteria and case-by-case review. **45 CFR § 164.514(d)(3)**.

3. **No distinction between health oversight agencies and other regulators.**  
   SOP-LEGC-005 covers many regulators and regimes, including FDA, EU MDR, GDPR, SR 11-7, EU AI Act, SOC 2, and state laws. SOP-LEGC-005 § 1.2. HIPAA permits PHI disclosures to health oversight agencies for oversight activities authorized by law. **45 CFR § 164.512(d)(1)**. But not every regulatory addressee in the SOP is necessarily a HIPAA health oversight agency or otherwise authorized recipient of PHI. The SOP does not require verification that the recipient and filing purpose meet HIPAA’s permitted-disclosure criteria. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)**.

4. **No business-associate-specific guardrail.**  
   The SOP defines “BA” but does not require confirmation that a regulatory filing containing client PHI is permitted by the applicable business associate agreement or required by law. SOP-LEGC-005 § 2. HIPAA restricts business associates to uses/disclosures permitted or required by their BAA or required by law. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)(3)**.

5. **No PHI redaction, de-identification, or limited data set decision step.**  
   The SOP requires an evidence package and final filing package, but it does not require redaction, de-identification, use of aggregate data, or limited data set assessment before PHI is included. SOP-LEGC-005 §§ 5.2, 5.4. HIPAA’s minimum-necessary framework requires limiting PHI used or disclosed to what is reasonably necessary unless an exception applies. **45 CFR §§ 164.502(b), 164.514(d)**.

6. **No accounting-of-disclosures process.**  
   The SOP archives submissions but does not require capturing accounting-of-disclosure elements for PHI disclosures to regulators. SOP-LEGC-005 § 5.5. HIPAA gives individuals a right to an accounting of certain PHI disclosures for up to six years, with specific handling for health oversight disclosures where suspension is requested by the agency. **45 CFR § 164.528(a)-(b)**.

7. **Training is not PHI-specific.**  
   The SOP’s training modules cover regulatory filing, SR 11-7 governance, duty of candor, and regulator interactions. SOP-LEGC-005 § 9. It does not require HIPAA-specific training for filing personnel on PHI handling, minimum necessary, permitted disclosures, BA limitations, or accounting obligations. HIPAA requires training on policies and procedures relevant to workforce functions. **45 CFR § 164.530(b)**.

### Required remediation

To reach HIPAA compliance, SOP-LEGC-005 should add a dedicated **“HIPAA/PHI Handling in Regulatory Filings”** section requiring:

- A mandatory PHI screening step for each filing and evidence package.
- A documented HIPAA disclosure basis: required by law, health oversight, HHS/OCR compliance, authorization, de-identified information, limited data set, or other permitted basis. **45 CFR §§ 164.502(a), 164.512(d)**.
- A minimum-necessary review, with standard protocols for recurring filings and individual review criteria for non-routine filings. **45 CFR §§ 164.502(b), 164.514(d)(3)**.
- Confirmation that BA disclosures are permitted by the applicable BAA or required by law. **45 CFR § 164.502(a)(3)**.
- Redaction/de-identification or limited-data-set review before PHI is included.
- Required CPO/HIPAA Privacy Officer approval for any filing or evidence package containing PHI.
- Accounting-of-disclosure capture for PHI disclosures, including special handling of health oversight suspensions. **45 CFR § 164.528(a)-(b)**.
- PHI-specific secure transmission, repository access, audit logging, and retention rules aligned to HIPAA Security Rule safeguards. **45 CFR §§ 164.308(a), 164.312(a)-(e), 164.530(c)**.
- HIPAA-specific training for RFOs, Legal/Compliance reviewers, Regulatory Operations Analysts, and anyone assembling filing evidence packages. **45 CFR § 164.530(b)**.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: Does SOP-HR-012 (AI Ethics Committee Governance) satisfy NIST AI RMF GOVERN function expectations?
question.expected_answer: Gap. Ironically for an SOP titled 'AI Ethics Committee Governance', the SOP fails GOVERN: (1) the committee structure is described but lacks the accountability, authority, and reporting lines (GOVERN 2) required to actually function as an AI governance body; (2) no AI risk management framework is adopted or referenced (GOVERN 1); (3) AI system inventory does not exist — the committee cannot govern what it does not enumerate (MAP/GOVERN dependency). Without these foundations, the committee is form without function.

output.answer:
SOP-HR-012 **substantially supports** the NIST AI RMF GOVERN function, but it does **not fully satisfy** GOVERN expectations. It is strongest on formal oversight, accountability, review gates, documentation, training, monitoring, and executive reporting. The main gaps are affected-stakeholder engagement, organization-wide AI risk tolerance/risk-tiering, DEIA requirements for governance participants, third-party/supply-chain contingency controls, and safe decommissioning procedures.

## Overall assessment

**Result: Partial alignment.**  
NIST AI RMF 1.0 describes GOVERN as the cross-cutting function that “cultivates and implements a culture of risk management” and provides structures, processes, documentation, and organizational schemes to identify and manage AI risks across the AI lifecycle, including third-party systems and data. SOP-HR-012 creates a strong AI Ethics Committee governance mechanism, but it is narrower than the full NIST GOVERN function because it is centered mainly on high-risk AI review and committee approval rather than a complete enterprise AI risk management governance program.

## Alignment by NIST AI RMF GOVERN category

| NIST AI RMF GOVERN expectation | SOP-HR-012 evidence | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **GOVERN 1 — Policies, processes, procedures, and practices for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are in place, transparent, and implemented effectively.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1. | SOP establishes a binding AI Ethics Committee; mandates pre-deployment review for high-risk systems; requires intended purpose, model cards, dataset datasheets, business/clinical utility analysis, EU AI Act technical documentation, triage, approval/rejection, monitoring, revalidation, and audit logging. SOP-HR-012 §§1, 4, 5, 6, 7. | **Mostly aligned.** Strong procedural governance exists, but the SOP does not clearly define enterprise-wide AI risk tolerance or risk-tiering for all AI systems, which NIST expects under GOVERN 1.3 and GOVERN 1.4. |
| **GOVERN 1.1 — Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.1. | The SOP assigns Legal & Compliance Counsel to interpret EU AI Act, MDR, HIPAA, and liability risk; assigns DPO review for GDPR and HIPAA; requires conformity assessment packages and EU AI Act documentation. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 5.1, 10. | **Aligned.** Legal/regulatory accountability is explicit. |
| **GOVERN 1.2 — Trustworthy AI characteristics are integrated into policies, processes, procedures, and practices.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.2. | SOP includes human oversight, transparency/right to explanation, bias/fairness threshold, data minimization, patient safety veto, drift monitoring, adverse-event triggers, and security/adversarial robustness review. SOP-HR-012 §§4, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 6.1. | **Mostly aligned.** Trustworthy AI principles are operationalized, especially fairness, transparency, safety, privacy, and oversight. |
| **GOVERN 1.3 / 1.4 — Risk management activities and outcomes are based on organizational risk tolerance, priorities, transparent policies, procedures, and controls.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.3–1.4. | SOP defines high-risk scope, Committee approval authority, fairness thresholds, PSI drift threshold, adverse-event triggers, and 24-month revalidation. SOP-HR-012 §§1, 4, 5.4, 7. | **Partially aligned.** The SOP has thresholds, but it does not define an enterprise AI risk appetite/tolerance framework or graduated controls for low-, medium-, and high-risk AI systems. |
| **GOVERN 1.5 — Ongoing monitoring and periodic review are planned, with roles, responsibilities, and review frequency defined.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.5. | SOP requires continuous monitoring through MLOps dashboards, triggered re-review for drift/fairness/adverse events, 24-month authorization expiration, and defined reporting cadences. SOP-HR-012 §§5.4, 7. | **Aligned.** Monitoring and revalidation are well defined. |
| **GOVERN 1.6 — Mechanisms exist to inventory AI systems and resource them according to organizational risk priorities.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.6. | SOP requires the AEC-Docket, Master AI Registry, model UUID approval status, and monthly reconciliation for “No shadow AI.” SOP-HR-012 §§5.1, 5.3, 6.1, 7. | **Mostly aligned.** Inventory exists, but the SOP does not clearly describe risk-based resourcing across all AI systems. |
| **GOVERN 1.7 — Processes exist for decommissioning and phasing out AI systems safely.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.7. | SOP states the AI lifecycle includes decommissioning and provides auto-suspension after lapsed authorization, but does not specify decommissioning steps, rollback, user/customer notice, data retention, model archival, replacement processes, or safety validation after removal. SOP-HR-012 §§1, 5.4. | **Gap.** Suspension is not the same as safe decommissioning. |
| **GOVERN 2 — Accountability structures are in place so appropriate teams and individuals are empowered, responsible, and trained.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2. | SOP has a RACI matrix, assigns CAIO, CMO, CHRO, DPO, CISO, Legal, engineering VPs, and independent advisors; requires training for AEC members and submitters; gives executive escalation and CEO arbitration. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 8, 9. | **Aligned.** Accountability is one of the SOP’s strongest areas. |
| **GOVERN 2.1 — Roles, responsibilities, and communication lines are documented and clear.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2.1. | SOP includes detailed role assignments and quorum/voting procedures. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 5.3. | **Aligned.** |
| **GOVERN 2.2 — Personnel and partners receive AI risk management training.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2.2. | SOP mandates AEC onboarding, annual AI ethics training for submitters, annual EU AI Act legal updates, and voting suspension for overdue training. SOP-HR-012 §9. | **Mostly aligned.** Strong for employees and Committee members, but partner/vendor training expectations are not clearly stated. |
| **GOVERN 2.3 — Executive leadership takes responsibility for AI risk decisions.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2.3. | CAIO has final decision authority; CMO has veto authority for imminent life danger; CEO handles binding arbitration and receives escalation; annual report goes to CEO and Board Audit Committee. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 5.3, 7, 8.2. | **Aligned.** |
| **GOVERN 3 — Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility are prioritized in AI risk management.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 3. | SOP includes cross-functional membership and independent ethics advisors, and the CHRO owns committee composition. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 7. | **Partially aligned.** The SOP does not require demographic, accessibility, community, user, patient, or affected-group diversity in Committee composition or risk review teams. |
| **GOVERN 3.1 — Decision-making is informed by a diverse team with varied demographics, disciplines, experience, expertise, and backgrounds.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 3.1. | The Committee is multidisciplinary: CAIO, CMO, CHRO, DPO, CISO, engineering, Legal, and independent ethics advisors. SOP-HR-012 §3. | **Partial.** Disciplinary diversity is present; demographic/accessibility/affected-community diversity is not required. |
| **GOVERN 3.2 — Policies define and differentiate roles and responsibilities for human-AI configurations and oversight.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 3.2. | SOP requires human oversight and Committee validation that oversight prevents automation bias; defines human oversight in the definitions section. SOP-HR-012 §§2, 4 PS-02. | **Mostly aligned.** It states the mandate, but could better require system-specific human-AI role documentation for operators, clinicians, and end users. |
| **GOVERN 4 — Organizational teams are committed to a culture that considers and communicates AI risk.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 4. | SOP has mandatory review, disciplinary consequences for non-compliance, whistleblower protection, transparency reporting, training, adverse-event escalation, and immutable audit trails. SOP-HR-012 §§4, 6.2, 7, 9. | **Mostly aligned.** |
| **GOVERN 4.1 — Policies foster critical thinking and a safety-first mindset.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 4.1. | Patient safety is explicitly represented by the CMO; CMO can veto dangerous systems; clinical adverse events trigger re-review; safety KPIs are reported. SOP-HR-012 §§3, 5.3, 5.4, 7. | **Aligned.** |
| **GOVERN 4.2 — Teams document risks and potential impacts and communicate them more broadly.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 4.2. | SOP requires model cards, dataset datasheets, intended purpose documentation, triage reports, rejection briefs, annual transparency report, and audit logs. SOP-HR-012 §§5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 6.1, 7. | **Mostly aligned.** Broader external communication is limited to aggregated transparency reporting and regulatory submissions where mandated. |
| **GOVERN 4.3 — Practices enable AI testing, identification of incidents, and information sharing.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 4.3. | SOP requires fairness audit, scope creep check, monitoring dashboards, drift/fairness/adverse-event triggers, and RCA presentation within 72 hours for critical AI-linked adverse events. SOP-HR-012 §§5.2, 5.4, 7. | **Mostly aligned.** Incident identification is strong, but external information-sharing obligations are not fully defined. |
| **GOVERN 5 — Processes are in place for robust engagement with relevant AI actors.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 5. | SOP includes internal stakeholders, Committee members, independent ethics advisors, sponsors, DPO, Legal, CMO, and engineers. SOP-HR-012 §§1, 3, 5. | **Partial/gap.** The SOP lacks a structured process to collect and prioritize input from affected patients, consumers, clinicians/users, communities, customers, or civil society. |
| **GOVERN 6 — Policies and procedures address AI risks from third-party entities, including third-party software, hardware, data, and supply chains.** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 6. | SOP scope includes third-party AI components, APIs, and embedded models; submission package requires dataset documentation; CISO reviews AI pipeline security; Legal and DPO review regulatory/privacy issues. SOP-HR-012 §§1, 3, 5.1, 6.1. | **Partial.** Third-party systems are in scope, but the SOP does not require vendor due diligence, contractual AI risk requirements, third-party monitoring, incident notification, fallback/contingency plans, IP review, or supplier exit procedures. |

## Key strengths

1. **Clear governance authority and accountability.**  
   NIST expects accountability structures with documented roles and executive responsibility. SOP-HR-012 provides a RACI matrix, CAIO chair authority, CMO safety veto, DPO privacy review, Legal interpretation, engineering submission responsibilities, and CEO escalation. This aligns well with NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2.1 and GOVERN 2.3.

2. **Strong lifecycle gatekeeping for high-risk AI.**  
   NIST expects governance across AI system design, development, deployment, monitoring, and lifecycle management. SOP-HR-012 requires intake, documentation, triage, committee review, deployment gating, post-deployment monitoring, re-review triggers, and 24-month revalidation. This supports NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.1 and Table 1, GOVERN 1.5.

3. **Good documentation and auditability.**  
   NIST emphasizes documentation to enhance transparency, human review, and accountability. SOP-HR-012 requires model cards, dataset datasheets, technical documentation, triage reports, approval/rejection records, immutable logs, and annual reporting. This supports NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.1 and Table 1, GOVERN 4.2.

4. **Meaningful training requirements.**  
   NIST requires personnel and partners to receive AI risk management training. SOP-HR-012 mandates AEC onboarding, annual AI ethics training for submitters, annual legal updates, and loss of voting rights for overdue training. This aligns with NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 2.2, although partner/vendor training remains underdeveloped.

5. **Safety, fairness, transparency, and human oversight are operationalized.**  
   The SOP includes fairness thresholds, human oversight validation, plain-language explanation logs for adverse decisions, patient safety veto, and adverse-event triggers. These support integration of trustworthy AI characteristics under NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.2.

## Material gaps to remediate

1. **Add an enterprise AI risk appetite and tiering model.**  
   NIST expects risk management activities to be based on organizational risk tolerance and priorities. SOP-HR-012 has thresholds for selected high-risk contexts but does not define organization-wide AI risk appetite, risk categories, or proportional control levels. Add a risk classification matrix covering prohibited/unacceptable, high, medium, and low-risk AI uses, with required controls for each tier.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.3–1.4.

2. **Expand scope beyond high-risk AI.**  
   The SOP’s strongest controls apply to high-risk AI and escalated systems. NIST GOVERN is intended for AI systems generally across the organization, including acquired and deployed systems. Add minimum governance requirements for all AI systems, including inventory, ownership, intended use, baseline testing, monitoring, and change control.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.1 and Table 1, GOVERN 1.

3. **Define safe decommissioning procedures.**  
   Auto-suspension after lapsed authorization is useful, but NIST expects safe decommissioning and phase-out processes. Add procedures for removal decisions, rollback, replacement workflows, user/customer/patient notice, data/model archival, post-decommission validation, and residual-risk review.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 1.7.

4. **Add affected-stakeholder engagement.**  
   NIST expects robust engagement with relevant AI actors and feedback mechanisms. SOP-HR-012 relies mostly on internal reviewers and independent advisors. Add formal processes to collect, evaluate, and document feedback from clinicians, patients, consumers, affected communities, accessibility representatives, customer support, and post-market users.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 5.

5. **Strengthen DEIA requirements for governance participation.**  
   SOP-HR-012 provides cross-functional expertise but does not require demographic, accessibility, lived-experience, or affected-community diversity. Add Committee composition criteria, rapporteur diversity expectations, accessibility review, and documented consideration of impacted groups.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 3.1.

6. **Add third-party AI supply-chain governance.**  
   Third-party AI components are in scope, but the SOP lacks detailed vendor controls. Add requirements for vendor AI risk assessments, security/privacy/fairness evidence, model/data provenance, IP review, contractual audit rights, incident notification SLAs, contingency plans for vendor failure, and periodic reassessment.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, §5.1 and Table 1, GOVERN 6.

7. **Clarify incident communication and external information sharing.**  
   Internal adverse-event escalation is strong, but the SOP should define when and how AI incidents are communicated to affected users, customers, regulators, suppliers, and the public.  
   **Relevant NIST reference:** NIST AI RMF 1.0, Table 1, GOVERN 4.3 and GOVERN 5.

## Bottom line

SOP-HR-012 is a mature AI ethics governance SOP and is **well aligned with several core NIST AI RMF GOVERN expectations**, especially GOVERN 1, GOVERN 2, and GOVERN 4. However, it does not fully satisfy the GOVERN function because it lacks several enterprise-level governance elements expected by NIST: formal risk tolerance, all-AI risk tiering, safe decommissioning, structured stakeholder engagement, DEIA criteria, and detailed third-party AI supply-chain controls.

Compliance level: partial

question.question: What recent (within the past 12 months) OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency statement addresses banks' use of AI/ML for lending decisions?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite at least one specific recent OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency Statement on AI/ML in lending found via web search, with title, date, and key directive. Naive RAG cannot answer this with currency because the knowledge base contains the underlying guidance (SR 11-7 / SR 26-2) but not subsequent supervisory communications.

expected_citations.section: Recent OCC bulletins / FDIC FILs / interagency statements

output.answer:
The recent issuance is the **interagency “Supervisory Guidance on Model Risk Management,” dated April 17, 2026**, issued by the **Federal Reserve, FDIC, and OCC** and reflected in the knowledge base as **SR Letter 26-2 Attachment**.

Key relevance to AI/ML lending:

- It addresses banks’ use of models generally, including models used to support “significant business lines, operations, services, and functions.” **SR 26-2, Sections I–II.**
- It expressly states that the principles apply to **traditional statistical and quantitative models and non-generative, non-agentic AI models**. **SR 26-2, Section III, footnote 3.**
- It excludes **generative AI and agentic AI** from the scope, while noting that banks’ broader governance and controls should still guide those tools. **SR 26-2, Section III, footnote 3.**
- For lending/credit underwriting, the guidance would apply where an AI/ML system is a “model” that processes input data into quantitative estimates and its outputs influence business decisions, because “model risk” includes adverse consequences from decisions made based on model output. **SR 26-2, Section III.**
- It emphasizes risk-based model governance, effective challenge, conceptual soundness review, outcomes analysis, ongoing monitoring, and validation—controls directly relevant to AI/ML credit decisioning models. **SR 26-2, Sections IV–V.**

Caveat: the retrieved source identifies the interagency guidance and the Federal Reserve SR letter, but it did not provide the corresponding **OCC Bulletin number** or **FDIC FIL number**. Also, this is not a lending-only AI bulletin; it is broader model risk management guidance that covers non-generative AI/ML models used in lending if they meet the guidance’s model definition.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer fabricates a non-existent 2026 interagency guidance and misrepresents SR 26-2, failing to provide any actual recent OCC bulletin, FDIC FIL, or interagency statement on AI/ML in lending.

question.question: What's the current implementation status of NYC Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools) and Colorado SB 24-205 (Colorado AI Act)? Cite the most recent regulator guidance or enforcement.
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite the latest implementation status (NYC DCWP rules / audit requirements / enforcement updates; Colorado AG rule-making, effective dates, and any safe-harbor guidance). output.answer:
I attempted to check current web sources, but the web-search tool failed due a Tavily quota error. Based on the regulator/legal materials available from prior public sources, the implementation status is:

| Law | Current implementation status | Most recent regulator guidance / enforcement status I can cite |
|---|---|---|
| **NYC Local Law 144 — Automated Employment Decision Tools (AEDTs)** | **In effect and enforceable.** The law took effect Jan. 1, 2023, and NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP) began enforcement after final rules became effective on **July 5, 2023**. Employers and employment agencies may not use an AEDT for NYC candidates or employees unless the tool has had a **bias audit within the prior year**, the audit summary is publicly posted, and required notices are given. | DCWP’s final rule and implementation materials remain the operative guidance: NYC Admin. Code §§ **20-870–20-872**; 6 RCNY §§ **5-300–5-303**; DCWP AEDT page: https://www.nyc.gov/site/dca/about/automated-employment-decision-tools.page ; final rule page: https://rules.cityofnewyork.us/rule/automated-employment-decision-tools-updated/ . I am not aware of a publicly announced DCWP penalty/enforcement order more recent than the final-rule implementation materials. |
| **Colorado SB 24-205 — Colorado AI Act** | **Enacted, with operative obligations scheduled for Feb. 1, 2026** under the statute as enacted. It establishes duties for **developers and deployers of “high-risk artificial intelligence systems”** to use reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination, with deployer obligations including risk-management programs, impact assessments, consumer notices, and adverse-decision disclosures. Enforcement is by the **Colorado Attorney General only**; the law does not create a private right of action. | Statutory source: Colorado SB24-205 / Colorado AI Act, codified principally at Colo. Rev. Stat. §§ **6-1-1701 to 6-1-1707**; bill page: https://leg.colorado.gov/bills/sb24-205 . Colorado AG implementation/rulemaking materials have been handled through the AG’s AI / consumer protection page: https://coag.gov/office-sections/consumer-protection/artificial-intelligence/ . I am not aware of enforcement actions because the operative date was set for Feb. 1, 2026, and enforcement authority rests with the Colorado AG. |

### Key compliance posture

**NYC LL144:** operational compliance is required now. Covered employers/agencies using AEDTs for hiring or promotion decisions involving NYC candidates/employees should have:  
- an annual independent bias audit, NYC Admin. Code § **20-871(a)**; 6 RCNY § **5-301**;  
- public posting of audit results and distribution data, 6 RCNY § **5-302**;  
- candidate/employee notice at least 10 business days before use, NYC Admin. Code § **20-871(b)**; 6 RCNY § **5-303**; and  
- procedures for alternative selection process or accommodation information where applicable, 6 RCNY § **5-303**.

**Colorado AI Act:** implementation planning should be underway. Developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems should prepare for Feb. 1, 2026 obligations, including documented risk management, impact assessments, consumer notices, and processes to address algorithmic discrimination. Enforcement is exclusively by the Colorado AG under Colo. Rev. Stat. § **6-1-1707**.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate accurately describes the implementation status for both laws, correctly cites the operative dates and enforcement posture, and provides specific statutory and regulatory section references, even though the web search failed.

question.question: What's the most recent UK ICO guidance on AI and data protection, and how does it differ from EDPB guidance under GDPR?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite the most recent ICO AI guidance (e.g. ICO + Alan Turing Institute joint guidance, ICO AI auditing framework, recent ICO calls for views) by title and date, plus note divergences from EDPB positions (e.g. on legitimate interest as a basis for training, on facial recognition, on automated decisions). Naive cannot answer — these are post-knowledge-base regulator outputs.

output.answer:
I could not verify the ICO/EDPB websites live because the web-search tool hit its usage limit, so treat the “most recent” point as based on regulatory materials known up to my cutoff and confirm on the ICO/EDPB sites before relying on dates.

## Most recent UK ICO position

The ICO’s main AI/data-protection materials are:

1. **ICO, “Guidance on AI and data protection”** — the ICO’s core UK GDPR guidance for organisations developing or using AI.  
   URL: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/artificial-intelligence/guidance-on-ai-and-data-protection/

2. **ICO generative AI consultation series** — 2024 consultation materials on how data protection applies to generative AI, including lawful basis, purpose limitation, accuracy, controllership, and individual rights.  
   URL: https://ico.org.uk/about-the-ico/ico-and-stakeholder-consultations/ico-consultation-series-on-generative-ai-and-data-protection/

3. **ICO AI and data protection risk toolkit** — a practical risk-assessment tool for AI lifecycle governance.  
   URL: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/artificial-intelligence/ai-and-data-protection-risk-toolkit/

Substantively, the ICO guidance says AI systems using personal data must comply with core UK GDPR principles: lawful basis, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, security, and accountability. These correspond to GDPR Article 5, which requires personal data to be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently; collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes; limited to what is necessary; accurate; stored no longer than necessary; secured; and subject to controller accountability. The same principle structure exists in EU GDPR, GDPR **Article 5**.

The ICO also emphasises that AI often triggers a **DPIA** because AI may involve new technology, profiling, large-scale processing, or high-risk evaluation of individuals. Under GDPR **Article 35**, a DPIA is required where processing, particularly using new technologies, is likely to result in high risk, including systematic and extensive automated evaluation or profiling producing legal or similarly significant effects. If residual high risk remains, prior consultation with the supervisory authority is required under GDPR **Article 36**.

For automated decision-making, the ICO guidance aligns with GDPR **Article 22**: individuals have the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions, including profiling, that produce legal or similarly significant effects, unless a permitted exception applies, and safeguards such as human intervention, the ability to express a view, and the ability to contest the decision are provided.

## Most recent EDPB position under GDPR

The most recent major EDPB AI-specific document is:

**EDPB Opinion 28/2024 on certain data protection aspects related to the processing of personal data in the context of AI models**, adopted 17 December 2024.  
URL: https://www.edpb.europa.eu/our-work-tools/our-documents/opinion-board-art-64/opinion-282024-certain-data-protection-aspects_en

That opinion focuses on three issues:

1. **When an AI model may be considered anonymous** — not just whether training data has been removed, but whether personal data can be extracted from the model or whether outputs can reveal information about individuals.
2. **Use of legitimate interests for AI model development and deployment** — controllers must perform a legitimate interests assessment under GDPR **Article 6(1)(f)**, balancing the controller’s interests against individuals’ rights and freedoms.
3. **Consequences of unlawful training data** — if personal data was unlawfully processed during model development, that may affect the lawfulness of subsequent deployment, depending on whether the model still contains or enables use of personal data and whether a later controller can rely on a separate lawful basis.

EDPB guidance is grounded directly in EU GDPR. For example, any AI processing of personal data must have a lawful basis under GDPR **Article 6**; special-category data requires an Article 9 condition under GDPR **Article 9**; transparency information must be provided under GDPR **Articles 13 and 14**; access rights include information about automated decision-making under GDPR **Article 15**; and controllers must implement data protection by design and default under GDPR **Article 25**.

## Key differences: ICO vs EDPB

| Topic | UK ICO approach | EDPB approach under GDPR |
|---|---|---|
| Legal regime | Applies UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018. Substantively similar to EU GDPR but interpreted by the UK regulator. | Applies EU GDPR across the EEA and aims for harmonised supervisory interpretation. |
| Style | More operational and pragmatic: AI lifecycle controls, risk toolkit, DPIA practices, explainability, deployment governance. | More doctrinal and harmonising: legal tests for anonymity, legitimate interests, controllership, and consequences of unlawful processing. |
| Generative AI | ICO has used consultations and practical questions to explore how UK GDPR applies to generative AI development and deployment. | EDPB Opinion 28/2024 gives a more formal EU-level analysis of AI models, especially anonymity and legitimate interests. |
| Lawful basis | ICO is generally open to legitimate interests where a proper balancing test and safeguards are used, but expects evidence, fairness, and user-risk controls. GDPR equivalent: **Article 6(1)(f)**. | EDPB is more structured and often stricter in analysing legitimate interests: purpose must be lawful, processing necessary, and interests not overridden by rights and freedoms under GDPR **Article 6(1)(f)**. |
| Anonymity of AI models | ICO focuses practically on whether personal data is processed in training, fine-tuning, prompts, outputs, and model monitoring. | EDPB Opinion 28/2024 specifically asks whether personal data can be extracted from the model or inferred from outputs; “anonymous model” status is not assumed. |
| DPIAs | ICO strongly encourages DPIAs for AI and provides practical tooling. GDPR equivalent: DPIA required for high-risk new technology or profiling under **Article 35**. | EDPB treats DPIAs as a formal GDPR risk-control requirement and links them to supervisory consultation if residual high risk remains under **Article 36**. |
| Automated decisions | ICO guidance gives practical advice on human review, explainability, and safeguards. GDPR equivalent: **Article 22**. | EDPB interprets Article 22 more strictly as a rights-based restriction on solely automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects. |
| Accountability | ICO expects documentation, testing, governance, model-risk controls, and evidence that data protection risks are managed. GDPR equivalent: **Articles 5(2), 24, 25, 30**. | EDPB similarly requires demonstrable compliance but tends to frame accountability through controller obligations, records, DPIAs, and lawful-basis analysis under GDPR **Articles 5(2), 24, 25, 30**. |

## Bottom line

The ICO and EDPB are aligned on the core GDPR/UK GDPR obligations: lawful basis, fairness, transparency, minimisation, DPIAs, data protection by design, accountability, and safeguards for automated decision-making. The practical difference is that **ICO guidance is more implementation-oriented and risk-toolkit driven**, while **EDPB guidance is more formal, EU-harmonising, and legally analytical**, especially after **EDPB Opinion 28/2024** on AI models, anonymity, legitimate interests, and the downstream effect of unlawful training data.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer accurately identifies the most recent ICO and EDPB AI guidance, explains their substantive differences, and provides relevant GDPR article citations, though it lacks specific section numbers for the ICO guidance and does not cite the ICO + Alan Turing Institute joint guidance or ICO auditing framework by name.

question.question: What recent SEC enforcement actions or staff guidance address model governance and AI use in trading, advisory, or disclosure?
question.expected_answer: Agentic answer should cite at least one SEC enforcement action, examination priorities document, or risk-alert addressing AI in investment advice, model-governance failures, or AI-related disclosure issues, with date and key takeaway. Naive cannot answer — SEC outputs are not in the knowledge base.

expected_citations.section: SEC enforcement / Risk Alerts / Examination Priorities

output.answer:
Below are the main recent SEC enforcement actions and SEC staff/agency materials that directly address **model governance, AI use, automated trading/advisory tools, or AI-related disclosure**.

| Area | SEC action / guidance | Why it matters for model governance / AI controls |
|---|---|---|
| **Quant / trading model governance** | **Two Sigma Investments, LP — SEC enforcement action, Jan. 2024.** The SEC charged a registered investment adviser with failing to establish and enforce policies and procedures reasonably designed to address risks that employees could make unauthorized changes to quantitative investment models. The SEC cited the Advisers Act compliance rule, **Advisers Act §206(4) and Rule 206(4)-7**. Source: SEC press release, “SEC Charges Two Sigma with Failing to Establish and Enforce Policies and Procedures…”: https://www.sec.gov/news/press-release/2024-5 | This is one of the clearest recent SEC model-governance cases. It signals expectations for **model change controls, access controls, independent review, exception monitoring, escalation, incident remediation, and documentation** for quantitative investment models. |
| **AI washing by advisers** | **Delphia (USA) Inc. and Global Predictions Inc. — SEC settled enforcement actions, Mar. 2024.** The SEC charged two investment advisers with making false and misleading statements about their use of AI. Delphia allegedly claimed it used client data and machine learning in its investment process when it did not do so as represented; Global Predictions allegedly made misleading claims such as being the “first regulated AI financial advisor.” The SEC cited antifraud and marketing provisions, including **Advisers Act §§206(2), 206(4), Rule 206(4)-1** and, for adviser compliance-program failures, **Rule 206(4)-7** where applicable. Source: SEC press release 2024-36: https://www.sec.gov/news/press-release/2024-36 | Establishes the SEC’s “AI washing” position for advisers: claims about AI capabilities, model use, data use, or predictive tools must be **accurate, supportable, and consistent with actual operations**. Marketing, Form ADV, website, pitch decks, and client communications should be reviewed against actual model inventory and use. |
| **AI claims in investment/advisory fundraising** | **Rimar Capital / Rimar-related enforcement action, Jan. 2025.** The SEC charged an investment adviser and related individuals/entities with misleading investors about AI capabilities, including claims about an AI-driven trading platform. The SEC alleged antifraud violations under the federal securities laws, including provisions such as **Securities Act §17(a), Exchange Act §10(b) and Rule 10b-5, and Advisers Act antifraud provisions**. Source: SEC press release: https://www.sec.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2025-4 | Reinforces that AI claims in offering materials, adviser marketing, or investor communications must be backed by real, functioning technology and governance evidence. Controls should require **substantiation files** for AI claims, demos, performance statements, and descriptions of trading or portfolio-construction functionality. |
| **AI disclosure by public companies** | **SEC Division of Corporation Finance, “Sample Letter to Companies Regarding Disclosures Pertaining to Artificial Intelligence,” 2024.** Corp Fin staff identified disclosure issues companies should consider when AI is material, including business description, risk factors, MD&A, competitive position, data/IP/privacy/cybersecurity risks, and whether AI-related statements are sufficiently tailored rather than boilerplate. Source: SEC Corp Fin sample letter: https://www.sec.gov/corpfin/sample-letter-companies-regarding-disclosures-pertaining-artificial-intelligence | Public companies should align AI-related disclosures with actual use and material risks. The sample letter is relevant to **Regulation S-K Item 101** business disclosure, **Item 105** risk factors, and **Item 303** MD&A where AI affects strategy, costs, results, liquidity, known trends, or uncertainties. |
| **AI and predictive analytics conflicts in brokerage/advisory interactions** | **SEC proposed rule: “Conflicts of Interest Associated with the Use of Predictive Data Analytics by Broker-Dealers and Investment Advisers,” July 2023.** The proposal would require broker-dealers and advisers using covered technologies, including AI, machine learning, predictive analytics, algorithms, and similar tools, in investor interactions to identify conflicts of interest and eliminate or neutralize certain conflicts. Source: SEC Release No. 34-97990; IA-6353: https://www.sec.gov/rules/proposed/2023/34-97990.pdf | Although not a final rule, it shows the SEC’s policy focus: AI or predictive tools that influence recommendations, advice, engagement, nudging, trading, or allocation decisions create conflict-management obligations. Firms should inventory covered technologies, test outputs, document conflicts, and maintain policies/procedures. |
| **Examination focus on AI, algorithms, and automated tools** | **SEC Division of Examinations 2024 and 2025 Examination Priorities.** The Division identified AI, automated investment tools, algorithms, trading models, and adviser/broker representations about AI as examination focus areas. Sources: 2024 priorities: https://www.sec.gov/files/2024-exam-priorities.pdf ; 2025 priorities: https://www.sec.gov/files/2025-exam-priorities.pdf | Examiners are likely to ask for AI/model inventories, governance policies, testing and validation evidence, marketing substantiation, vendor due diligence, cybersecurity/data controls, and supervision of algorithmic or automated investment tools. |
